coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules derived from pineapple juice: A potential sensing probe for specific and selective detection of chromium (VI) ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Shelja
Umar, Ahmad
Mehta, Surinder Kumar
Kansal, Sushil Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report, for the first time, the facile, green and one step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice as a sole carbon source without involving any strong acid treatment. The synthesized CNG not only exhibits hydrophilicity and can remain stable for several weeks but also demonstrates prominent blue color fluorescence under UV light (λ=365nm). These CNG showed wide range of emission spectra in the visible region depending upon the different excitation wavelengths. The synthesized CNG were characterized in detail in terms of their morphological, structural, optical, compositional and thermal properties using numerous analytical and spectroscopic techniques. For application point of view, the perspective to utilize CNG as fluorescent sensing probe has also been explored with the introduction of various highly toxic metal ions and interestingly, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of CNG was drastically quenched by chromium (VI) ions with high selectivity over the linear range of concentrations 0–18µM with stern volmer constant 2.29×104 M−1. Such encouraging results revealed that simply prepared CNG are potential scaffold to fabricate highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensors for the detection of highly hazardous and life threatening metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon
Fluorescence
Sensing probe
Chromium (VI) ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
Characterizations of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
Fabrication of fluorescent Cr (VI) ions sensor based on fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
Impact of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
PL quantum yield (QY) measurement of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
Morphological and structural properties of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
Optical properties of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
Characterization of Cr (VI) ions fluorescent sensor based on fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules
Selectivity of the fabricated Cr (VI) ions fluorescent sensor based on fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the synthesis of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice.
Typical (a) XRD pattern and (b-d) FESEM images of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice.
Typical HRTEM images of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice.
Typical (a) thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and (b) FTIR spectrum of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice.

Typical (a) Raman-scattering spectrum and (b) UV–vis spectrum of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice. Inset (b) shows the photograph of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) in UV light.
Typical XPS spectra of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice; (a) full scan; (b) C 1s and (c) O 1s spectra.
Typical room-temperature PL spectra of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules at different excitation wavelengths.
Up conversion PL spectra of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (a) at an excitation wavelengths from 500nm to 710nm (b) at an excitation wavelengths from 730nm to 870nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CCNGs=0.25µgmL−1) in the presence of different amounts of Cr6+(10−1 M to 10−6 M) at excitation wavelength of 370nm, (b) Linear response at low concentration of Cr6+ with respect to fluorescence intensity.
Fluorescent intensity change of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules in the presence of different metal cations (0.1M, volume 20µL) at an excitation wavelength of 370nm.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules derived from pineapple juice: A potential sensing probe for specific and selective detection of chromium (VI) ions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies, Panjab University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Promising Centre for Sensors and Electronic Devices (PCSED), Najran University, P.O. Box-1988, Najran 11001, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Promising Centre for Sensors and Electronic Devices (PCSED), Najran University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering & Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering & Technology, Panjab University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Science and Arts, Najran University, P.O. Box-1988, Najran 11001, Saudi Arabia.
Corresponding author at: Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering & Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report, for the first time, the facile, green and one step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice as a sole carbon source without involving any strong acid treatment. The synthesized CNG not only exhibits hydrophilicity and can remain stable for several weeks but also demonstrates prominent blue color fluorescence under UV light (λ=365nm). These CNG showed wide range of emission spectra in the visible region depending upon the different excitation wavelengths. The synthesized CNG were characterized in detail in terms of their morphological, structural, optical, compositional and thermal properties using numerous analytical and spectroscopic techniques. For application point of view, the perspective to utilize CNG as fluorescent sensing probe has also been explored with the introduction of various highly toxic metal ions and interestingly, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of CNG was drastically quenched by chromium (VI) ions with high selectivity over the linear range of concentrations 0–18µM with stern volmer constant 2.29×104 M−1. Such encouraging results revealed that simply prepared CNG are potential scaffold to fabricate highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensors for the detection of highly hazardous and life threatening metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Numerous varieties of carbon nanomaterials have been flourished for ages and their promising and remarkable performance makes them capable enough to be applied in a wide spectrum of fields [1]. Recently, new ranges of carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, nanoballs, nanospheres, fullerenes, nanodots, nanofibers and so on, have been widely studied as a contemporary class of carbon materials with advanced properties that diversify significantly from the basic forms of carbon [2–8]. The advancement in terms of perceiving the properties and the chemistry of carbon nanomaterials has unlocked an entire world of technological applications of nanomaterials. Owing to their extraordinary electrical, optical, thermal, physiochemical and mechanical properties, these nanostructures tend to exhibit enormous potential in vast areas of biomedical field, photocatalysis, photoelectronic, drug delivery, sensors, energy storage and conversion and many other important applications [9–15]. In particular, carbonaceous nanomaterials, primarily constituted of major amount of carbon with trace presence of oxygen and nitrogen, display strong fluorescence properties, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, broad excitation spectra, narrow and tunable emission spectra, good biocompatibility, outstanding water solubility, low toxicity, and therefore are advantageous over heavy-metal-based semiconductor quantum dots which cause environmental hazard and long term toxicity concerns [16,17]. For instance, the fluorescent emission of carbon nanomaterials shows the unique excitation- dependent nature in comparison to semiconductor based quantum dots [18].

Various top down and bottom up strategies have been adopted to synthesize carbon nanomaterials, including laser ablation, electrochemical and chemical oxidation, thermolysis and microwave assisted approaches [19–24]. Nevertheless, these procedures have some shortcomings since these not only require undesirable severe synthetic and harsh conditions, pre or post hazardous acidic treatment, high temperature but also are time-consuming processes and cost ineffective, which restrict their wide applicability. Anilkeeva et al. synthesized incorporated carbon globules using reactive polymer as precursor [25]. Monodisperse and colloidal type carbon nanospheres were synthesized by Pan and coworkers in large scale from polyoxometalates via hydrothermal approach [26]. Recent research in this field demonstrated that a number of “green” raw materials have been used as carbon precursors for synthesizing carbon based nanomaterials. Sahu et al. fabricated highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal reaction of readily available natural bio-resource, i.e. orange juice [27]. De et al. demonstrated an inexpensive and green approach of preparing water dispersible carbon nanodots by carbonization of banana juice [28]. Wu et al. prepared luminescent carbon nanodots from commercial food grade honey [29]. Yin et al. demonstrated a green and simple method for the preparation of photoluminescent carbon particles by carbonizing sweet pepper at low temperature [30]. However, the yields obtained from these resources were comparatively low and the process was tedious.

The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the carbon nanostructures are the most fascinating features with λex dependent emission intensity and position [31]. It has been found that the PL intensity of carbon based nanoparticles can be diminished by the introduction of various metal ions in the respective aqueous solutions at different extent by assisting the non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes through an efficient photoinduced charge migration mechanism. Based on this phenomenon, various studies have been carried out to quantify different metal ions selectively [31–36]. Heavy metal ion pollution has evolved as one of the most critical global issue for years because of the serious threats not only to human health but also to the environment. Owing to its toxicity, carcinogenicity and long perseverance in the environment; the handling of heavy metal ions becomes a challenging task and thereby straightforward, susceptible and sensitive determination approaches are of great need. Till now, this characteristic feature of fluorescent carbon nanostructures has been practiced for developing such analytical techniques which can reliably detect toxic metals ions with ease.

In the current research work, we have devised, for the first time, a novel, facile and green hydrothermal method to synthesize large quantity carbon nanoglobules (CNG) derived from pineapple juice as a sole carbon source without involving any strong acid treatment. The prepared CNG exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent PL behavior with a high quantum yield of about ~10.06% at the strongest emission around 453nm. Further, the synthesized fluorescent CNG were used as sensing probe for the detection of various highly toxic metal ions and interestingly, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of CNG was drastically quenched by chromium (VI) ions with high selectivity over the linear range of concentrations.

Pineapple (Ananascomosus) was procured from the general market. Ethanol, acetone, potassium dichromate and ethylene diamine (DEA) were purchased from Merck. Inorganic salts such as ferrous chloride, magnesium acetate, sodium chloride, strontium nitrate, lead nitrate and silver chloride were procured from Merck, India. Cadmium sulfide, copper sulfate, zinc sulfate, aluminium sulfate and dichloromethane were obtained from S. D. Fine Chem. Ltd (India). Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment. Entire chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification.

The solution of Cr (VI) was prepared from K2Cr2O7. Cr (VI) stock solution of 10−1 M was prepared and the other concentrations of Cr (VI), i.e. 10−2 M, 10−3 M, 10−4 M, 10−5 M and 10−6 M were prepared by diluting the original stock solution of 10−1 M Cr (VI). Further, the exact volumes of these solutions were utilized in the analytical procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
ce:section.ce:para: CNG were prepared using the facile hydrothermal approach. In a typical synthesis experiment, 100mL of fresh pineapple juice (pulp free and without adding any preservative) was mixed with 75mL ethanol under stirring for 15min and the resultant mixture was placed in Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for 3h at 150°C. After desired reaction time, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room-temperature. Subsequently, a dark brown solution was obtained which was further washed with dichloromethane thoroughly to remove other unreacted organic species. The two different organic and aqueous layers were separated with the help of washing. In the upper organic layer, the excess acetone was added and was further centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min to eliminate the unreacted precipitates. The supernatant was extracted and the precipitates were discarded. Finally, a yellow solution of CNG was obtained which was further passivated with DEA solution at 100°C for 72h after sonicating for 10min. Finally, dark brown powder was obtained which was characterized by means of various techniques to determine its structural, morphological, thermal and optical properties and finally used to fabricate highly sensitive fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
ce:section.ce:para: Various techniques were used to characterize the prepared CNG. The general morphologies of the prepared sample were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM; JEOL-JSM-7600 F) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; TECNAI- 42200 STWIN (SEI HOLAND)). The structural and crystal properties were examined by powder X- ray diffraction (XRD; PANanalytical XpertPro.), with Cu-Kα radiation (λ=1.54178Å) at 45kV and 40mA in the 2θ ranging from 10° to 80°. The chemical composition of the prepared sample was determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Thermoscientific Nicolet iS50) equipped with a diamond ATR at room-temperature with KBr pellet as reference ranging from 450 to 4000cm−1. The absorption spectrum of the prepared sample was obtained using a UV Spectrophotometer (Systronics -2202) while the room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) analysis was done on PL spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000; 5J1-004 model) using quartz cuvette 1cm path length. Thermogravimetric analysis was performed in the temperature ranging from 0 to 1000°C with an acceleration speed of 10°Cmin−1 using (TGA/DSC analyzer; Perkin- Elmer STA 6000). Raman-scattering analysis of the prepared sample was recorded by Renishaw via Raman microscope using Ar laser of excitation wavelength 514nm. X- ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using Kratos axis ultra DLD employing a monochromatic Al- Kα X- ray source (hυ=1486.6eV). Zeta potential was measured by a particle size analyzer (Horiba SZ-100). The time resolved fluorescence spectrum of the sample was carried out using Edinburgh FL920 Fluorescence lifetime spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent Cr (VI) ions sensor based on fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensing of Cr (VI) with CNG was carried out as followed: About 2mL of CNG solution (concentration=0.25µg/mL) was filled into the cuvette. The certain equivalents of Cr (VI) were introduced stepwise to the CNG solution using micropipette. The final volume of solution remained intact as a very minute quantity of Cr (VI) was introduced into it. This solution mixture was then incubated for half a minute and then was excited at 370nm. The fluorescence emissions for corresponding excitation values were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of various metal cations such as Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+,Sr2+,Pb2+, Na+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Al3+ and Cu2+ have been prepared in distilled water. All the dilute samples were prepared from standard stock aqueous solution of concentration of 0.1M of their respective metal ions. Briefly, an aqueous suspension of metal salt was mixed with 2mL of CNG solution (concentration=0.25µg/mL) and incubated for half min. The fluorescence spectra of the resultant solution were carried out at excitation wavelength (λex=370nm) and excitation and emission slit 10nm at PMT 700V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL quantum yield (QY) measurement of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
ce:section.ce:para:
The PL QY of obtained CNG was calculated using sulfuric acid as a standard (reference) material. The following equation was used to calculate the QY:
Q
S
=
Q
R
×
(
I
S
/
I
R
)
×
(
A
R
/
A
S
)
×
(
η
2
/
η
2
R
)
where QS and QR are QYs of the prepared sample and standard respectively, IS and IR are the integrated PL emission areas of CNG and the standard material respectively, AS and AR are the absorbance values of the CNG and the standard material at an excitation wavelength of 370nm, and η and ηR are the refractive indices of the solvents. The PL QY of the CNG excited at 370nm was calculated to be 10.061%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural properties of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophilic CNG were synthesized by a thermal carbonization of pineapple juice as illustrated in Fig. 1. The plausible mechanism for the formation of CNG involves hydrothermal carbonization of the prime constituents of pine apple juice such as sucrose, glucose, fructose, citric acid and ascorbic acid. The carbohydrate contents present in juice undergo dehydration and decomposition in the presence of citric acid and ascorbic acid during the hydrothermal treatment, thereby resulting in the synthesis of various solvable aldehydic and ketonic compounds along with several organic acids such as formic acid, propionic acid and acetic acid etc. It is noteworthy to mention that the hydronium ion composed from these acids acts as a catalyst in different reaction steps. Further, various soluble polymeric products were obtained by the polymerization, condensation and aromatization of the products via aldol condensation. When the concentration of aromatic reached the point of supersaturation, a burst nucleation took place and CNG were formed [28].

The X- ray diffraction pattern of as synthesized CNG revealed a wide diffraction peak, which is centered at 2Ѳ=19°attributed to (002) plane of a graphitic structure of carbon ( Fig. 2a). The interlayer spacing d=0.34nm was indicating the imperfect crystallization and turbostratic carbon nanostructures [37] due to the generation of functional groups containing more oxygen on the surface. The surface structure and morphology of the synthesized products were examined by FESEM which revealed that nanoglobules shaped carbon materials were synthesized in large quantity (Fig. 2b). The sample consisted of clustered and aggregated globular carbon nanoparticles having almost spherical morphology with relatively regular size and homogeneity [38]. Moreover, due to high-density growth, some agglomeration was also seen in the synthesized product (Fig. 2c). It was clearly observed from the high-resolution FESEM images that the synthesized nanoglobules possessed spongy and porous structures as indicated by white arrows (Fig. 2d).

The typical diameter of the spongy nanoglobules is in the range of 150±25nm. To understand the detailed morphological properties, the synthesized nanoglobules were examined by TEM and the observed results are shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 3 exhibits the typical TEM images of as-synthesized CNG which comprehends that the nanoparticles are possessing spherical shape, typically symbolize amorphous carbonaceous structure (Fig. 3a) without any recognizable lattice spacing as reported earlier [39,40]. The TEM images clearly revealed that the nanoglobules are spongy and porous. Moreover, due to high-density growth, some agglomeration in nanoglobules is seen. The observed TEM images are well-consistent with the FESEM analysis. Fig. 3b exhibits the typical high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of nanoglobules which shows that the there is no sign of any discernible lattice structures suggesting an amorphous nature of as-synthesized CNG.

The amount and rate of variation in weight of material with the function of temperature was determined by TGA under controlled environment. A different amount of weight losses with respect to temperature indicates the thermal stability of the sample. As shown in Fig. 4a, it was observed that CNG were stable up to 120°C and minute weight loss before 120°C was due to loss of adsorbed water over the surface. Further, there was another significant weight loss at 452°C which corresponded to the decomposition of chemical moieties attached to the surface of CNG via covalent linkages [41]. The functional groups and chemical composition of CNG were investigated by FTIR analysis (Fig. 4b). The FTIR peaks at 3275 and 1645cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of -OH group and C=O of amide group respectively; the peaks at 2896, 2981 and 875cm−1 corresponded to the bending, stretching and rocking band vibrations of C-H bond. On the other hand, the peaks at 1373cm−1 and 1095cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of C-O-C group, respectively [42].

The Raman spectrum of the CNG displayed two dominant peaks at around 1359cm−1 and 1600cm−1, ascribed to the D-band (sp3) and G-band (sp2), respectively ( Fig. 5a). The D-band was related to the vibrations of carbon atoms along with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. The G-band was corresponded to the E2g mode of graphite and was related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms [43,44].

The ratio of intensities of ID/IG was also determined and it was calculated to be ~0.75, indicating an amorphous nature of CNG and is consistent with reported studies [45,46]. Fig. 5b represents the corresponding UV–vis spectrum for CNG. The as synthesized CNG exhibited a characteristic absorption band at ∼268nm, corresponded to the π–π* electronic transitions of conjugated C=C sp2 domains, having a tail extrapolating towards the visible region. Besides, another shoulder peak at 280nm could be attributed to n-π* transition of the carbonyl group (C=O) [47]. The synthesized CNG in aqueous solution displayed blue color emission under the exposure of UV light as shown in Inset Fig. 5b.

The surface configuration of CNG was investigated by XPS analysis. XPS measurements demonstrated that the sample was mainly consisting of carbon and oxygen ( Fig. 6a). The two predominant peaks at about 285eV and 532.9eV as shown in the XPS wide survey spectrum were corresponding to the binding energies of C 1s and O 1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6b, in the high resolution spectrum of C 1s, three kinds of bonds were observed, ascribed to C–C, C–O and C=O groups. In the high resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 6c), the two prominent peaks at 531.9 and 533eV were observed, which were assigned to C=O, and C–OH/C–O–C groups, respectively [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules (CNG)
ce:section.ce:para: The characteristic mark of CNG is its tunable PL emission spectra and size based PL behavior. PL is one of the most fascinating features of CNG from the integral as well as application point of view. At different excitation wavelength ranging from 330 to 430nm, the PL spectra of CNG were recorded ( Fig. 7). With an excitation wavelength of 370nm, a protruding PL emission peak at 453nm was obtained.

The PL emission peaks were also bathochromically moved with the escalation of the excitation wavelength. The mechanism for such PL behavior of CNG is quite intricate and its exact origination has not been eventually stated yet. The possible explanation for it is the existence of different size of particles in the surface energy traps of CNG [50]. Also, the red shift in PL emission peak is because of the disparity in distribution of size of CNG. In this manner, the small sized particles get excited at a lower wavelength, while large sized particles get excited at higher wavelengths. Another factor, which influences the intensity of PL, is that how many particles got excited at that specific wavelength which inferred that the enormous amount of particles got excited at 370nm as maximum PL intensity of CNG was observed at that particular wavelength. Hence, 370nm was chosen as the optimum excitation for further experimental procedures. The surface structure and its nature also affected the excitation wavelength dependent PL activity of CNG. The different chemical groups attached covalently at the surface of the CNG resulted in a sequence of surface emissive traps amid π and π* of the conjugated ethylenic bond.

Upon illumination at a particular excitation wavelength, PL emission phenomenon occurred due to the surface state energy trap. With the variation in excitation wavelength, the alternative surface emissive traps turned out to be foremost prevalent. On that account, the optical properties are attributed to both the carbon core and chemical groups or solely by fluorophores [51]. Accordingly, the PL mechanism relies upon both the varied size distribution and surface state defects.

The PL lifetime of CNG was determined using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurement. The PL time decay curve was obtained by exciting the sample of CNG at 370nm and by detecting the emission intensity at 453nm, fitted with three exponential functions with time constants t1=0.149ns, t2=0.7379ns and t3=4.4ns, giving an average decay about tavg =1.76ns [34]. Upconversion fluorescence is well-defined as the emission photon energies which are comparatively greater than those of the excitation photon energies. The materials possessing upconversion PL have tendency to transform a longer wavelength irradiation i.e. near infrared light to shorter wavelength irradiation i.e. visible light, and display promising potential in catalysis and energy conversion applications [43]. Remarkably, CNG possessed clear upconversion PL properties, apart from displaying strong fluorescence in UV-to-near-infrared range. When excited from 500nm to 710nm, the upconversion PL was emitted at almost similar wavelength with maximum emission at 453nm, suggesting that the emission range falls in visible region with bathochromic shift as shown in Fig. 8a. Upconversion PL spectra of as-synthesized fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules at excitation wavelengths from 730nm to 870nm with increments of 20nm also showed the emission in visible region as displayed in Fig. 8b, along with hyperchromic shift. This upconverted PL property of CNG might be ascribed to the multiphoton active process [52].

Zeta potential indicates the charge and stability of colloidal dispersions. The zeta potential of CNG was found to be −29.7mV at pH=9. This value indicated the anionic charge on the surface of CNG. The average particle size of CNG was determined by DLS to be ~7nm. The enhanced average particle size of CNG as compared to HRTEM is due to the fact that DLS considered the complete hydrodynamic diameter of the nanomaterial, including particles as well as the adsorbed molecules and ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Cr (VI) ions fluorescent sensor based on fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the serious threat of Cr6+ ions on environment and human health, it is obviously imperative to develop a proficient detection technique which can monitor the level of these ions in aqueous environment. Considering that this CNG have been functionalized with –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 groups on the surfaces, we anticipate for exploring the feasibility of adopting CNG for well meeting the requirements of a fluorescent probe to detect Cr6+ ions.

Towards that determination, Cr6+ ions were introduced into the aqueous suspension of as synthesized hydrophilic CNG. It was witnessed that the fluorescence intensity of CNG was diminished substantially with in few seconds of contact time in a concentration dependent manner. This rapid response was highly desirable for the ions detection. This suggested that Cr6+ ions initially quenches the fluorescence of CNG by binding to it, which affected the surface states of CNG, resulting in the charge transfer from CNG to Cr6+.

Upon the addition of various concentrations of Cr6+ to CNG of a fixed concentration (0.25µg/mL), the fluorescence intensity changes were investigated upon the excitation at 370nm as displayed in Fig. 9a. Initially, blank CNG exhibited a characteristic fluorescence emission band at 450nm.

Upon the addition of Cr6+ions, significant fluorescence quenching could be observed as the fluorescence intensity of CNG quenched gradually with the increasing Cr6+concentration due to the binding of Cr6+ ions to the surface of CNG (Fig. 9a). Zheng et al. also reported the determination of Cr6+ ions by fluorescence quenching of carbon nanoparticles of concentration 0.25µg/mL with one minute incubation time at excitation wavelength of 370nm [53].

Fig. 9b displayed the linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity of CNG and concentration of Cr6+ as stated by the stern- volmer equation given below:
I
0
/ I = 1
+
K
sv
[ Q ]
where I0 is the fluorescence intensity of aqueous solution of CNG and I is the fluorescence intensity of CNG with Cr6+ ions, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of quencher, i.e. Cr6+ ions and Ksv is the stern volmer constant.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the analysis was carried out in duplicate and error bars have also been plotted. The obtained calibration curve showed the linear relationship (R2=0.9956) of I0/I versus the concentration of Cr6+ over the range of 0–18µM with lowest detection limit 0.052µM and Ksv was found to be 2.29×104 M−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the fabricated Cr (VI) ions fluorescent sensor based on fluorescent spongy carbon nanoglobules
ce:section.ce:para: These results exhibited that the CNG could perform as fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of Cr6+ in the aqueous media. In addition to the sensitivity experiment, selectivity experiments were also carried out as these are of equal importance for the detection. To examine the selectivity of nanoprobe, the change in ratio of fluorescence intensities in the presence of various metal cations such as Ag+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, K+, Na+, Sr2+,Pb2+, and Zn2+ under the similar experimental conditions were recorded as shown in Fig. 10. Only the addition of Cr6+ led to the significant quenching of the fluorescence intensity. It could be stated that CNG could act as fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of Cr6+ with high selectivity even in the presence of other interfering metal cations, due to the binding of Cr6+ ions at the surface of CNG, resulted from charge transfer. This sensitive and selective determination of Cr6+ ions and the corresponding fluorescence quenching process exhibited that CNGs can be utilized as highly efficient turn –off fluorescent probe for Cr6+ ions.

Hydrophilic CNG were fabricated through hydrothermal approach using a green precursor, pine apple juice, which is low cost, non toxic and readily available. It displayed fascinating excitation wavelength and concentration dependent PL activity in the visible range. They were found to be excellent and highly efficient fluorescent probes for harmful chromium ions detection. The detection by this “turn- off” fluorescent sensor was accomplished by fluorescence quenching mechanism. It can be regarded that the current strategy may provide a new milestone for developing such cost effective, sensitive and efficient Cr+6 sensors for environmental remediation applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support received from the UGC – BSR Grant, Government of India through a grant No. F.25-1/2013(BSR)/5-91/2007 (BSR) and Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF), Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing the required instrumentation facilities. Ahmad Umar would like to acknowledge Promising Centre for Sensors and Electronic Devices (PCSED) granted by the Ministry of Education, Saudi Arabia to Najran University, Saudi Arabia.
